Topic 5: Part 1 (the Kharg Island strategy & market control)

[194:56]I do want to get into our war with Iran. We've got some big developments for you tonight

[195:02]And where we left off we are in a stalemate in the war with Iran week number five is starting

[195:10]We have been in this war now for exactly four weeks and there is no end in sight. It is a true catastrophe

[195:18]And we've talked a lot about why that is.

[195:21]The United States and Israel have engaged Iran in a joint operation using only air and

[195:27]sea power.

[195:28]They were hoping for a quick and decisive strike, so overwhelming that it would topple the regime,

[195:34]that the masses of Iranians would rise up and change their government.

[195:39]And this is what the US and Israel, maybe more so the United States, was counting on.

[195:47]The Iranian regime is proved to be resilient.

[195:50]And although we killed its leader and much of its leadership, although we have crippled

[195:54]its military and we've bombed them almost 10,000 times, the Iranian regime remains intact.

[196:01]And, remaining intact, they still retain the capability to launch drones and missiles

[196:09]at the Gulf Cooperation Council nations, at US military bases, at Israel, and maybe most

[196:15]importantly at shipping the transits through the Strait of Hormuz.

[196:20]So, the United States is effectively unable to achieve its strategic objectives.

[196:26]If its strategic objective was to quickly and decisively collapse the Iranian regime,

[196:32]that goal now appears to be out of reach.

[196:35]However, we have initiated hostilities, Iran has retaliated, and they have changed the

[196:42]dynamic of how the region works.

[196:44]By closing the strait, they have effectively seized control of the global energy market.

[196:50]20% of the world's energy that transits by ship goes through the strait, and Iran is

[196:56]effectively controlling it via a toll.

[197:03]And the countries that are allied with Iran have safe passage and Iran's oil itself is

[197:10]able to be exported and nations that are willing to pay a $2 million toll, they're able to

[197:17]get their goods through the straight as well.

[197:19]But countries that are antagonistic towards Iran, countries at war with Iran or allied

[197:25]with those who were with Iran, they're being attacked.

[197:29]And so the conflict is settled in to an attritional war,

[197:33]where the United States is unable to complete its objectives.

[197:37]Meanwhile, in open hostilities,

[197:39]Iran is choking off the supply of global energy,

[197:42]which is badly hurting the US economy,

[197:46]our allies in the Indo-Pacific and the economy of Europe.

[197:50]So Trump is faced with a couple of options.

[197:54]He can escalate the war and how the war is being fought to ultimately achieve his objectives

[198:01]He can in some way or another

[198:03]With an invading ground force with a nuclear strike those seem to be the only options

[198:09]He can escalate the tactics in the fighting to ultimately achieve his aims, which is a regime change

[198:16]They will be able then to suppress Iran's ability to attack the straight and the war will be one

[198:23]The alternative is he can deescalate by achieving a ceasefire or a truce.

[198:29]He can get Iran to stop choking off the straight through diplomacy and this would be considered

[198:35]a tactical retreat and ultimately a strategic victory for Iran.

[198:41]He doesn't want to do either of these things.

[198:43]These are two bad options.

[198:45]If we withdraw, if we surrender, Iran is going to be able to extract concessions and they

[198:52]If we escalate, the means by which we do so would be unacceptable, politically, geopolitically,

[199:00]can't use a nuclear weapon, unprecedented, can't invade too many casualties, not enough

[199:07]political capital, no morale, appetite for that domestically.

[199:16]this.

[199:19]If he doesn't succeed and Iran closes the straight, what's the exit plan?

[199:25]He doesn't seem to have one.

[199:26]And so as the days and weeks go by, this attritional war is hurting the U.S. economy.

[199:32]Oil prices are higher than ever.

[199:34]Stock market is having another very bad week.

[199:37]S&P 500 is down 500 points at close earlier tonight.

[199:46]Trump is trying to figure out a third way,

[199:49]a way to escalate the conflict,

[199:52]maybe not achieving total victory,

[199:54]but a decisive blow, a tactical victory,

[199:58]that brings Iran to the negotiating table

[200:01]so that we can achieve favorable terms

[200:03]for a withdrawal with dignity,

[200:11]and how he's decided to do this is to engage Iran's energy industry in a very tactful way.

[200:20]So far, that seems to be the elephant in the room. The only way to hurt Iran any further

[200:26]without actually invading Iran

[200:29]We seem to be doing as much as we can with air and sea power, dropping bombs from above,

[200:34]launching missiles from ships.

[200:36]It seems that the only other way we can escalate short of a ground invasion is to begin targeting

[200:42]Iran's energy infrastructure, which we have avoided doing so far.

[201:58]And the third way appears to be not destroying Iran's energy infrastructure, but taking control over it.

[202:06]And so he's fixated on this small island called Karg Island, as you know, in the Persian Gulf.

[202:13]And although the oil is extracted and refined on the coastline of Iran's mainland,

[202:20]The actual oil is pumped out via pipeline to the island,

[202:25]95% of it, where it is then loaded onto a ship

[202:29]and then exported.

[202:30]And so that seems to be the critical choke point

[202:33]for Iran's entire energy industry,

[202:35]which accounts for half of its exports.

[202:39]95% of the oil and the oil is 50% of Iran's exports.

[202:44]So Trump believes that he doesn't necessarily have to destroy Iran's energy infrastructure

[202:51]if he can seize control over it, he can take it hostage and then use that as a counterweight

[202:58]to how Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz. So to that end, two weeks ago, he bombed Karg Island,

[203:07]bombed its military assets, not their energy infrastructure, but just the military assets

[203:13]In the ensuing two weeks, it seems that he's building up a military force to take over

[203:20]that island.

[203:21]Invading Iran on the mainland would be almost impossible because of the terrain, the size

[203:28]of Iran's armed forces, the scale of its missile and drone stockpile.

[203:33]If we tried to invade Iran's mainland, it might be a suicide mission.

[203:42]If we can use air power and electronic warfare

[203:46]and other means to shut down that island,

[204:20]If you release ours, if you release a straight of Hormuz, we will release Carg Island back

[204:25]That seems to be the play.

[204:30]The United States does not want Iran to know that this is coming, that this is happening.

Topic 6: Part 2 (Trump's repeated stalling, escalation, & Iran's only stand)

[204:42]This is a nutritional war.

[204:44]It's an economic war.

[204:46]And part of the economic war, it's not just about the actual scarcity of the commodities

[204:51]involved, which is oil, natural gas, fertilizer, aluminum, other things.

[204:59]And so, the demand for these commodities and how they value them in the present is dependent

[205:07]upon how people perceive the flow of the conflict.

[205:12]If they think the war is going to go on for a long time, and if they think this energy

[205:16]crunch is not going to end anytime soon, it makes the economic problems much more acute

[205:22]right now.

[205:23]If they think, however, that everything is going according to plan and the war is going

[205:27]to be over any day and that there's going to be an influx of oil next week or in two

[205:33]weeks, then without all the panic, it makes the economic problems less severe.

[205:39]So it seems that simultaneously, Trump is preparing the way for a major escalation in

[205:45]the conflict, an actual ground invasion, not of Iran's mainland, but of its islands.

[205:50]It's a huge risk, major gamble.

[205:53]There's a big risk of American lives.

[205:55]And it seems that that might be the only way out.

[205:58]That might be threading the needle between a disgraceful retreat, which gives Iran a

[206:03]strategic victory and an unacceptable escalation.

[206:07]At the same time that he's preparing for this, he has to reassure the markets that this is

[206:12]not about to happen.

[206:15]And so that brings us to this past weekend and where we left off on Tuesday.

[206:21]Last weekend, Trump made a big ultimatum, Trump said on Saturday that if Iran does not

[206:27]open the Strait of Hormuz and end the war in 48 hours, then Trump would begin targeting

[206:33]Iran's power plants and its energy grid.

[206:36]Iran immediately replied predictably, and they said in the event that that happens, Iran

[206:46]And that's Qatar, that's the United Arab Emirates,

[206:49]that's Kuwait, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia.

[206:52]And Iran said, not only will we attack their energy,

[206:56]their LNG, their liquefied natural gas, their oil,

[206:59]but we'll also attack their desalination plants,

[207:01]which they rely on for drinking water.

[207:04]We'll also attack their hotels, airports.

[207:08]We'll attack, all their civilian infrastructure

[207:10]will be on the table.

[207:12]And in response to this news, everything on the market tanked, gold, Bitcoin, stock market.

[207:20]This would be an apocalyptic conflict for the economy, not just for oil and natural gas,

[207:27]but for everything.

[207:27]This would seriously tank the economy.

[207:35]days because Trump said that he was gonna give Iran an opportunity to make a deal, that

[207:40]there was gonna be this peace process, and he was gonna give them an opportunity to find

[207:47]And so he was extending the deadline.

[207:49]The news from today and this week is that on Wednesday, Trump said the deadline is extended

[209:40]with Iran and the United States

[209:42]taking out maximalist demands

[209:44]that are unacceptable to each other side,

[209:47]said the mediators.

[210:17]There's a political dimension to how conflict works.

[210:35]their leadership. We assassinate their supreme leader or half their military and civilian

Topic 7: Part 3 (Iran's control & the only options)

[210:52]to kill Iran.

[210:54]We went for the jugular.

[210:55]Make no mistake about it.

[210:57]This was intended to be the killing blow.

[211:01]This was not a nicky-knack surgical strike,

[211:04]wasn't a tactical hit.

[211:06]This was a definitive, decisive killing blow,

[211:10]or at least intended to be.

[211:12]And when they say decisive decision to cut,

[211:16]to come down,

[211:18]They mean, we're going to topple the regime, it's going to be decisive.

[211:22]On the other side of this blow, there's going to be a different regime, and it failed.

[211:28]Now that it failed, Iran has done the only thing that they can, which is to close the

[211:32]Strait of Hormuz.

[211:34]Now, and this is the problem that we're in, we're realizing we can't finish the job

[211:39]now.

[211:40]We took our shot, we missed, and now it's gonna be impossible to do what we initially

[211:46]set out to accomplish.

[211:47]And why is that?

[211:49]Because when you try to strike a killing blow, it actually has a rally around the flag effect.

[211:55]We killed the symbol of their regime, and so what this does is it emboldens everybody

[212:00]in the regime.

[212:02]All the hardliners, all the people that said not to trust the United States, all the people

[212:07]that really believe, death to America, really believe in the Shiite eschatology, they're

[212:13]now in control.

[212:14]And if they weren't true believers before, they are now.

[212:18]So they're dug in and they're entrenched because they're facing an existential danger.

[212:29]We're carrying out regime change with assassination strikes. We're literally threatening to murder

[212:34]all of them. So this is now about survival. This is not a negotiation war as politics

[212:42]by other means. For them, this is now existential. They're literal backs as human beings are

[212:47]up against a wall. They're facing certain death. So they have to win or they die.

[212:54]A regime that has to win or die does not negotiate.

[212:59]They're not going to meet in Pakistan.

[213:03]They're not going to hang out and talk about concessions, at least not right away.

[213:07]They want to guarantee their own survival.

[213:10]And so what they've done in response to this is closed the Strait of Hormuz and they have

[213:15]let it rain on the Gulf countries.

[213:17]They're now inflicting the maximum amount of pain.

[213:21]Why do they have to inflict the maximum amount of pain?

[213:24]Why do they have to take these steps,

[213:26]bombing the Emirates a thousand times,

[213:29]bombing Saudi Arabia, closing the strait,

[213:31]many ways turning other countries against them?

[213:35]They have to do this to reestablish deterrence.

[213:40]They have to do this in a word

[213:42]so that the United States doesn't try to kill them again.

[213:45]The United States tried to kill them, they failed.

[213:48]They can't succeed now.

[213:50]because with all the air and sea power,

[213:53]they're not gonna defeat all the 200,000 Iranian guard corps.

[213:57]They're not going to incite an uprising.

[213:59]They're not gonna be able to defeat every guy with a gun

[214:01]that is in control of the infrastructure

[214:04]and institutions of Iran.

[214:06]So now these people that have just survived

[214:08]the killing shot, they have to make sure

[214:10]that the United States can never attack again.

[214:12]How do you do that?

[214:13]Impose the heaviest cost possible.

[214:17]And because Iran does not have strategic weapons that can hit the United States, they have

[214:22]to do it in other ways.

[214:23]They have to act like a porcupine as, as a weaker adversary.

[214:27]And they have to make it as painful as possible for everybody.

[214:30]They have to make it a drag.

[214:32]They have to make it unpleasant and painful so that when the United States backs off

[214:37]unable to achieve its goals, it thinks twice before they try to kill Iran again.

[214:45]But now the United States is they're also in a bit of a bind. They also have to make a certain type of decision here

[214:52]So we tried to attack Iran. We failed if we now retreat and Iran

[214:58]Reestablishes deterrence then we look weak by comparison and then other countries that we are actually holding in

[215:26]And they think that because we have deterred China from intervening.

[215:33]If they invade Taiwan, you're in a war with the United States.

[215:36]It's a threat of force that is credible, that is strong.

[215:40]You don't want to mess with us.

[215:43]has a nuclear arsenal, they have some ambitions of their own.
